text.title: Embedding Models, Chunking, and Similarity
text.summary: Model selection guide, chunking rules, cosine similarity, and dimension reduction trade-offs for OpenAI embeddings.

rule.statement: Use text-embedding-3-small for most tasks (20x cheaper than ada-002 with better quality); only switch to text-embedding-3-large when retrieval recall is measurably insufficient after benchmarking.
rule.rationale: text-embedding-3-small at $0.02/M tokens vs. text-embedding-3-large at $0.13/M tokens represents a 6.5x cost difference. Quality gap is negligible for most semantic search tasks.

rule.statement: Never mix embedding model or dimensions between ingestion and query — incompatible vectors produce random similarity scores with no error.
rule.rationale: Embedding a query with text-embedding-3-small at 1536 dims against a corpus embedded with text-embedding-3-large at 3072 dims produces meaningless cosine similarity values.

rule.statement: Chunk documents at semantic boundaries (paragraphs, sections) with ~10% overlap; always chunk before embedding — input silently truncated at 8191 tokens produces poor-quality embeddings for the truncated portion.
rule.rationale: Fixed-token chunking at sentence boundaries loses semantic coherence. Overlap prevents missing information at chunk boundaries. Silent truncation produces embeddings that represent only part of the content.

rule.statement: Store both the embedding vector and the original chunk text; cache embeddings for static content — never re-embed unchanged text.
rule.rationale: Embeddings cannot be reversed to recover text. Agents that store only vectors have no way to return the source text in search results. Re-embedding static content wastes money and time.

example.code:
import numpy as np from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()
def embed(texts: list[str], model="text-embedding-3-small", dims=1536) -> list[list[float]]: resp = client.embeddings.create(model=model, input=texts, dimensions=dims) return [d.embedding for d in resp.data]
def cosine(a, b) -> float: a, b = np.array(a), np.array(b) return float(np.dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b)))
def top_k(query_emb, corpus_embs, corpus_texts, k=5): scores = [(cosine(query_emb, e), t) for e, t in zip(corpus_embs, corpus_texts)] return sorted(scores, reverse=True)[:k]
example.note: Normalize embeddings before storing — cosine similarity on normalized vectors equals dot product, which most vector DBs optimize for.

rule.statement: For RAG retrieval, embed a rewritten/expanded version of the user query, not the raw message; expanded queries improve recall for short or ambiguous inputs.
rule.rationale: Short queries (2–5 words) embed into sparse regions of the vector space that miss relevant documents. Query expansion via LLM rewrites them into semantically richer representations.
